root.title: Root: Prosecution Evidence Sufficiency Against VP Sara Duterte
root.question: Will the prosecution be able to present sufficient evidence against Vice President Sara Duterte during her impeachment trial?
root.direction: Assess the impeachment case by examining: (1) the specific allegations and charges, (2) the evidence the prosecution has or claims to have, (3) the legal standards and procedural requirements for impeachment in the Philippines, and (4) expert and public assessments of case strength.
root.success_criterion: Clear picture of the charges, available evidence, legal requirements, and informed assessments of prosecution's case strength.

children.title: Impeachment Charges and Allegations
children.question: What are the specific impeachment charges and allegations against Vice President Sara Duterte?
children.direction: Identify the formal articles of impeachment filed, the specific grounds (culpable violation of Constitution, treason, bribery, graft and corruption, other high crimes, betrayal of public trust), and the factual allegations underlying each charge.
children.success_criterion: Complete list of charges with underlying factual allegations and constitutional grounds cited.

children.title: Confidential Funds Misuse Allegations
children.question: What evidence exists regarding alleged misuse of confidential funds by VP Duterte?
children.direction: Search for reports on the P125 million confidential funds controversy, Commission on Audit findings, disbursement records, documentation requirements violations, and any whistleblower testimony or documentary evidence.
research_areas: Timeline of fund releases and spending
children.success_criterion: Detailed accounting of confidential funds allegations with specific evidence items identified (audit reports, missing documentation, testimony).

This article needs to be updated. The reason given is: to reflect the second impeachment. Please help update this article to reflect recent events or newly available information. (May 2026)
Sara Duterte, the 15th and current vice president of the Philippines, has faced allegations regarding the use of confidential funds allocated to the Office of the Vice President (OVP) and the Department of Education (DepEd) during her tenure as Secretary of Education.

Background[edit]

Sara Duterte[edit]
Sara Duterte is the vice president of the Philippines under Bongbong Marcos. Duterte was appointed as Secretary of Education, serving from June 30, 2022, until her resignation on July 19, 2024.[1] She cited the government's alleged "mishandling" of the national budget for DepEd as among the reasons for her resignation.[2] As vice president, Duterte heads the Office of the Vice President (OVP).

Confidential funds[edit]
Confidential and intelligence funds are part of the national budget and are intended for activities that cannot be publicly disclosed, including surveillance and intelligence operations. Their allocation and use have been subject to scrutiny, particularly when assigned to civilian agencies.[3][4]

As per Joint Circular No. 2015-01, the two funds involve:[5]

Confidential expenses – related to surveillance activities in civilian government agencies supporting their mandate or operations.
Intelligence expenses – related to intelligence-gathering activities of uniformed personnel and other practitioners with direct impact on national security.

For 2024, Congress removed confidential funds from DepEd and the OVP.[6]

Confidential funds under Sara Duterte[4][7]

Allegations[edit]
The allocation for the OVP has received scrutiny since 2023 during deliberations for the 2024 national budget.[4] The Commission on Audit (COA) reported in 2023 that ₱112 million in confidential funds was spent within 11 days, raising concerns among lawmakers in the House of Representatives.[9]

In 2025, groups filed a plunder complaint alleging that Duterte had misused ₱612.5 million in confidential funds, including ₱500 million from the OVP and ₱112.5 million from DepEd.[10]

Reports also highlighted acknowledgment receipts containing names such as "Mary Grace Piattos", which were cited by lawmaker

children.answer: The Commission on Audit (COA) issued a disallowance notice for P73 million of the Office of the Vice President's P125 million confidential fund in 2022, citing illegal, irregular, or improper use according to documentation requirements (https://www.factrakers.org/post/ovp-s-p125m-confidential-fund-not-cleared-by-coa). The prosecution in VP Duterte's impeachment trial stated that alleged misuse of confidential funds would take the longest to present because it covers both the OVP and the Department of Education, which she led until mid-2024 (https://qa.philstar.com/headlines/2026/07/17/2542784/alleged-confidential-funds-money-trail-takes-center-stage-impeachment-trials-3rd-week). Additionally, plunder and graft charges related to confidential funds were filed against Duterte and 15 others before the Ombudsman in December 2025 (https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Sara_Duterte_confidential_funds_controversy). **Confidence: High** - multiple corroborating sources cite specific COA findings and ongoing legal proceedings.

children.answer: In November 2024, VP Sara Duterte made a videotaped statement threatening to kill President Ferdinand Marcos Jr., First Lady Liza Marcos, and former Speaker Martin Romualdez if she herself were killed; this video was presented as evidence in her Senate impeachment trial (gmanetwork.com, philstar.com). The National Bureau of Investigation (NBI) investigated these threats and, according to testimony by NBI Director Melvin Matibag in July 2026, found that Duterte had the "capacity to carry out" the death threats and determined there was "reasonable certainty of conviction" for grave threats and inciting to sedition (mb.com.ph, pna.gov.ph). Duterte herself later claimed the remarks were "not a threat" and merely hypothetical (philstar.com). Confidence: High - multiple sources confirm the recorded threat and subsequent investigation findings.

House of Representatives 
 
 Impeachment complaint filed against VP Sara Duterte
 
 
 Dec 2, 2024 3:01 PM PHT 
 Dwight de Leon
 
 

 

 
 
 

 
 
 
 WHY THIS MATTERS
 
 
 
 
 
 A coalition of organizations has filed an impeachment complaint against Vice President Sara Duterte, citing betrayal of public trust and misuse of public funds.
 The House of Representatives faces time constraints to process the complaint before the current lawmakers' term ends, with potential complications from President Marcos' appeal to not pursue the impeachment.
 Duterte has labeled the investigations as political persecution and has made controversial statements regarding threats to other officials.
 
 
 
 
 This is AI-generated and may have errors. For context, always refer to the full article. Report issues here. 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 VICE PRESIDENT. Vice President Sara Duterte attends the Senate hearing on the 2025 budget of the Office of the Vice President, on November 13, 2024. 
 
 Angie de Silva/Rappler 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 (1st UPDATE) Sara Duterte has been in hot water over her alleged misuse of public funds as head of the Office of the Vice President and as former education secretary
 

 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
MANILA, Philippines – A coalition of various organizations filed an impeachment complaint against Vice President Sara Duterte with the House of Representatives on Monday, December 2, kickstarting a long process that, if successful, could remove her from office. 

 
 
 
 
 

 
 

The groups, which include Magdalo, Mamamayang Liberal, Tindig Pilipinas, families of drug war victims, and leaders of the Catholic Church want Duterte indicted for betrayal of public trust, bribery, graft and corruption, culpable violation of the Constitution, and other high crimes.

“[Lawmakers] have to give this complaint a fair chance and a fair day in court. This impeachment complaint is important in starting the true process towards holding the Vice President accountable,” Akbayan Representative Perci Cendaña, who is endorsing the complaint, said.

“If not now, when? We believe that if there’s a will, there’s a way,” he added. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

The House has been investigating Duterte over her alleged misuse of public funds as head of the Office of the Vice President (OVP) and as a former secretary of the Department of Education, her Cabinet post under the Marcos administration for two years until her resignation in June this year.

One contentious issue is how her offices handled confidential funds worth hundreds millions of pesos. During a recent hearing, it was discovered that her security personnel managed the secret funds, even though government rules dictate that special disbursing officers should be the ones in charge of the money.

Former senator Leila de Lima, the spokesperson of the group, noted that the complaint listed 24 articles of impeachment. Aside from the failure to account for the confidential expenditures, the complaint wants to hold her to account for her alleged direct involvement in extrajudicial killings when she was Davao City mayor, and her assassination threats against President Marcos and Speaker Martin Romualdez, among others.

House Secretary General Reginald Velasco confirmed receiving the impeachment complaint later on Monday.

Sara Duterte impeachment 
 
 LIST: The 4 articles of impeachment vs VP Sara Duterte
 
 
 May 4, 2026 2:52 PM PHT 
 Dwight de Leon
 
 

 

 
 
 

 
 
 
 WHY THIS MATTERS
 
 
 
 
 
 The House justice committee approved a report on articles of impeachment against Vice President Sara Duterte on May 4.
 The report includes four articles of impeachment related to alleged constitutional violations, graft, corruption, and threats against government officials.
 The impeachment articles seek to declare Duterte guilty, disqualify her from public office, and hold her liable for prosecution.
 
 
 
 
 This is AI-generated and may have errors. For context, always refer to the full article. Report issues here. 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 IMPEACHMENT. Members of the House justice committee vote on the impeachment complaints filed against President Ferdinand Marcos Jr. on February 2, 2026. 
 
 House of Representatives Facebook 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 The impeachment articles request the impeachment court to declare Duterte guilty of all the offenses presented, disqualify her from public office, and declare her liable for prosecution
 

 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
The House justice committee approved its report on the articles of impeachment against Vice President Sara Duterte on Monday, May 4. 

The document, a copy of which was obtained by Rappler before the start of Monday’s proceedings, lists four articles of impeachment, a consolidation of the alleged offenses hurled at the vice president in the two impeachment complaints.

 
 
 
 
 

 
 

These are the following:

Article I: Culpable violation of the Constitution, graft and corruption, and betrayal of public trust

Related to her alleged misuse of confidential funds as head of the Office of the Vice President and the Department of Education worth a combined P612.5 million

Article II: Culpable violation of the Constitution and betrayal of public trust

Related to the supposed unexplained wealth, stemming from the discrepancy between her bank transaction records and her declared net worth in her Statements of Assets, Liabilities, and Net Worth (SALNs)

Article III: Bribery, graft and corruption, culpable violation of the Constitution, and betrayal of public trust

Related to her alleged monetary gifts to DepEd officials

Article IV: Culpable violation of the Constitution, high crimes, and betrayal of public trust

Related to her alleged threats to assassinate President Ferdinand Marcos Jr., First Lady Liza Araneta Marcos, and former speaker Martin Romualdez, and her supposed efforts to incite to sedition against the government 

The impeachment articles request the impeachment court to declare Duterte guilty of all the offenses presented, disqualify her from public office, and declare her liable to prosecution, trial, and punishment. – Rappler.com

‘PSA, COA records to prove fake confidential funds recipients’

By Maricel Cruz

July 17, 2026, 8:56 PM 

 

 
 
 Share
 FacebookTwitterPinterestWhatsApp
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 Atty. Benjamin Tolosa Jr., Bicol Saro party-list Rep. Terry Ridon and former Surigao del Norte Rep. Robert Ace Barbers from the House prosecution team hold a press conference on July 17, 2026. (Courtesy: House of Representatives) Resize Text:16px
 
 
 
 Estimated reading time: 3 minutes and 37 seconds

House prosecutors on Friday said they will present Philippine Statistics Authority (PSA) records, Commission on Audit (COA) reports, handwriting analysis, bank records, and witness testimony to support allegations that Vice President Sara Duterte misused confidential funds under the Office of the Vice President (OVP) and the Department of Education (DepEd).

House prosecutor and Bicol Saro Party-list Rep. Terry Ridon said the evidence will be presented under Article I of the Articles of Impeachment, which accuses Duterte of irregularly disbursing confidential funds.- Advertisement -

Among the evidence are PSA certifications intended to determine whether individuals listed as recipients of confidential funds actually exist.

“Ilalahad din doon ang PSA report kung saan sinasabi that these particular personalities do not actually exist (The PSA report stating that these specific individuals do not actually exist will also be presented),” Ridon said.

He said the names surfaced during congressional inquiries into acknowledgment receipts used to liquidate confidential funds released to the OVP and DepEd.

“They were never born, they were never married, they had never died in the Philippines,” Ridon said.

‘Mary Grace Piattos’ among names

Among the names questioned during the House inquiry were “Mary Grace Piattos” and “Milky Sikuya,” who were allegedly listed as recipients of confidential funds.

“We will discuss Mary Grace Piattos, Milky Sikuya, and other names that obtained or received confidential funds from the Office of the Vice President,” he said in Filipino.

Ridon said prosecutors will also present handwriting findings showing that acknowledgment receipts bearing different recipient names appeared to have been signed by the same individuals.

“We can discuss here the similar signatures of these individuals, where it was noted that it seemed only one group signed on their behalf,” he said.

He said the prosecution would combine the PSA records with handwriting analysis, bank evidence, and testimony on the release and liquidation of confidential funds to determine where the money went.

“So iyan po iyong ilan sa mga pwedeng mailatag diyan po sa confidential funds misuse,” Ridon said.“Mahalaga ito dahil pera ito ng tao (This is important because it is the people’s money,) he added.

‘COA audit opinion not proof of no irregularity’

Ridon also said an unmodified audit opinion issued by the Commission on Audit should not be interpreted as proof that the confidential fund expenditures were free from irregularities.

“Let us not mislead the public into thinking that just because there is an unmodified opinion, a particular office has a clean slate,” he said.

He explained that an unmodified opinion pertains to the fair presentation of an agency’s financial statements and does not certify that every transaction complied with laws and auditing rules.

 Philippine Daily Inquirer / 05:55 AM November 06, 2024

MANILA, Philippines — The Commission on Audit (COA) has confirmed that the Office of the Vice President (OVP) had sent the audit body more than 1,200 deficient acknowledgment receipts to explain its use of P125 million in confidential funds spent in 11 days in December 2022.
In a hearing by the House good government and accountability committee on Tuesday, the COA said the OVP submitted acknowledgment receipts with erroneous dates, unreadable names, and those bearing only signatures to justify payments out of the agency’s P125-million confidential fund for 2022.

 Article continues after this advertisement
 
 
 
 
 
 
Catalan is the third DepEd official who has testified to regularly receiving cash envelopes from Duterte. Former DepEd Undersecretary and head of procuring entity Gloria Mercado and bids and awards committee chair Resty Osias earlier said they were given every month P50,000 and P12,000-P15,000, respectively, by Fajarda.
Still no-show
Meanwhile, Fajarda and six other OVP officials skipped the House panel hearing on Tuesday on Duterte’s expenditures, particularly the confidential funds she requested during her first years in office.
The committee also noted that Duterte’s chief of staff, Zuleika Lopez, had left for Los Angeles on Monday night.
OVP spokesperson Michael Poa also informed the panel that he had left his post, claiming difficulty in further fulfilling his duties.

findings.result:
confidential funds 
 
 How OVP, DepEd disbursing officers violated rule on confidential funds
 
 
 Nov 25, 2024 8:51 PM PHT 
 Bonz Magsambol
 
 

 

 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 Officials of the Office of the Vice President (OVP), attend the congress committee on Good Governance and Public Accountability hearing on the budget utilization of the OVP and the Department of Education under Vice President Sara Duterte, on November 25, 2024. 
 
 HOR 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
Vice President Sara Duterte's disbursing officers claim that it was the security personnel, not them, who managed the confidential funds
 

 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
MANILA, Philippines – Vice President Sara Duterte‘s special disbursing officers (SDO) told a House panel on Monday, November 25, that millions of confidential funds of the Office of the Vice President (OVP) and the Department of Education (DepEd), when Duterte led the agency, were managed by their security personnel.

Edward Fajarda and Gina Acosta, SDOs of DepEd and OVP, respectively, told the House committee on good government that their only role were disbursing the funds to security personnel of both agencies — Colonel Dennis Nolasco and Colonel Raymund Dante Lachica, who is the head of the Vice Presidential Security and Protection Group (VPSPG).

According to Joint Circular No. 2015-01 issued by the Department of Budget and Management and the Commission on Audit (COA), it is the responsibility of the SDO, not security personnel, to disburse confidential funds.

Screenshot from the DBM-COA joint circular

Fajarda said he withdrew P37.5 million in confidential funds from the Land Bank of the Philippines and placed the money in two duffel bags and one backpack. He also said that he was disbursing the money to Nolasco on a weekly basis.
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

Former DepEd special disbursing officer Edward Fajarda testifies during the House committee on good government and public accountability hearing on the budget utilization of the OVP and the DepEd under Vice President Sara Duterte, on November 25, 2024. HOR photo

“Sa security officer lang po ako nagdi-disburse niyan. Wala na pong iba. Hindi po ‘yan buong P37.5 million na ibibigay sa kanya. Mostly weekly po yan. Weekly po. Nagre-range po sa P4 million to P6 million weekly,” Fajarda said. 

(I only disburse it to the security officer. No one else. The entire P37.5 million is given to him in one blow. It’s mostly on a weekly basis. Weekly. It ranges from P4 million to P6 million weekly.)

Fajarda also confirmed that he made three withdrawals of P37.5 million each, totaling P112.5 million, and that he was unaware of how the funds were used because it was not his “expertise.”

 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
“Hindi po kasi ako ang expert diyan. Si Colonel Nolasco po. Siya po kasi ang gumawa niyan,” he added.

(I’m not the expert on that matter. Colonel Nolasco is. He was the one who handled that.)

Fajarda said at the hearing that Lachica assigned a security officer named “Allan” to accompany him during the fund withdrawals.

Acosta, for her part, told the House panel that she did the same, handing over the P125 million in confidential funds to Lachica. She also said that she, along with OVP Assistant Chief of Staff Lemuel Ortonio, withdrew the money from LandBank and placed it in “traveling” bags. They were accompanied by two VPSPG drivers.

OVP chief of staff Zuleika Lopez (right) at House panel hearing Nov. 20, 2024. DANNY PATA The House prosecution panel will present Zuleika Lopez, the chief of staff of Vice President Sara Duterte as a witness in Duterte’s impeachment trial next week. ADVERTISEMENTHouse prosecutor and Iloilo Representative Lorenz Defensor on Wednesday informed the Senate impeachment court of the decision after the defense argued that Lopez’s detention in the House of Representatives and transfer to the Correctional Institute for Women in 2024 prompted Duterte to issue threats against President Ferdinand Marcos, Jr. and members of his family. Defensor said Lopez will take the stand on Tuesday, July 14. The prosecution said Lopez will be testifying on Article 4 of the Articles of Impeachment which accuses the Vice President of culpable violation of the Constitution, high crimes, and contracting betrayed public trust by contracting for the assassination of the President and his family members by making grave threats, and by actively inciting sedition against the republic.In a press conference shared via live video on social media on November 2024, Duterte said, “Huwag kang mag-alala, ma'am sa security ko, kasi may kinausap na ako na tao. Sabi ko sa kanya, kapag pinatay ako, patayin mo si BBM, si Liza Araneta, at si Martin Romualdez.”(Don't worry about my security because I have already spoken to someone. I told that person that if I am killed, kill BBM, Liza Araneta, and Martin Romualdez.)The Vice President added that she instructed this said person that in the event of her death, she said efforts must be made to kill the said officials, to which she claimed the person agreed to. Lopez, at that time, was in detention after she was cited in contempt by the House good government and public accountability panel over her “evasion” in answering questions from lawmakers as to how the Office of the Vice President’s confidential fund was being used. —RF, GMA News Tags: Zuleika Lopez, Sara Duterte, Sara Duterte impeachment trial, Lorenz Defensor

 Cristina Chi - Philstar.comJuly 17, 2026 | 1:09pm
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 Vice President Sara Duterte arrives before the start of the impeachment trial at the Senate of the Philippines in Pasay, Metro Manila on July 7, 2026.Noel Celis, pool / AFP ADVERTISEMENT
 MANILA, Philippines — Vice President Sara Duterte's alleged misuse of confidential funds will take the longest to present among the four articles in her impeachment trial because it covers two offices — her own and the Department of Education, which she led until mid-2024 — the prosecution said Friday, July 17.

Article I focuses on P612.5 million in confidential funds from the Office of the Vice President (P500 million) and DepEd (P112.5 million), which will take up at least 31 of the prosecution's overall 62 trial dates, half its entire allocation, and more than any other charge. 

House prosecution legal spokesperson Benjamin Tolosa Jr. said their witnesses will trace the full process of how the funds were spent, from the cash advances, to where the funds allegedly went, to the liquidation submitted to the Commission on Audit (COA), to the COA ruling eventually ordering P448 million returned.  

"There are so many documents involved in this... We have many witnesses whose testimony needs to be heard to explain the whole process," Tolosa said in mixed English and Filipino on Friday, July 17. 

But the panel will try to shorten the presentation, Tolosa said, by dropping witnesses once the essential points are proven, which it did for the grave threats article, where it waived six witnesses. 

The prosecution could begin presenting on confidential funds "as early as Wednesday," House prosecutor Rep. Terry Ridon (Bicol Saro) said, provided National Bureau of Investigation Director Melvin Matibag's direct and cross examination is completed on Tuesday.

 NBI witness confirms VP Sara death threat video in impeachment trial
 
 
 
 Philippines

 NBI witness confirms VP Sara death threat video in impeachment trial
 
 
 
 By Ellson Quismorio
 
 
 
 
 Published Jul 7, 2026 08:28 pm
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 At A Glance
 
 
 
 NBI Senior Agent John Mark Calilung testifies before the Senate impeachment court, authenticating video evidence of Vice President Sara Duterte's death threats against President Marcos and the First Family.
 

 
 

 Calilung details the forensic process, including screen recording, hash value generation, and preservation requests to Meta, while prosecutors present both the Nov. 23 and Oct. 18, 2024 recordings to support Article IV.
 

 
 

 Prosecutors emphasize that Duterte's repeated violent remarks raise constitutional questions on values expected of high officials, framing the threats as culpable violations and betrayal of public trust.
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 NBI Senior Agent John Mark Calilung (left), Vice President Sara Duterte (Senate PRIB)
 

 
 
 National Bureau of Investigation (NBI) Senior Agent John Mark Calilung was able to confirm before the Senate impeachment court on Tuesday, July 7 a video supporting the allegation that Vice President Sara Duterte made death threats to President Marcos and other members of the First Family.Calilung--a cybercrime investigator--was the first witness to be placed on the stand by the House prosecution panel tasked to gain a conviction on Duterte.Lawyer Amando Virgil Ligutan, a counsel for the House prosecutors,was the one who carried out direct examination on the witness during the second day of the high-stakes proceedings.The respondent made the now-infamous death threats during a virtual press conference on Nov. 23, 2024 while she was at the House of Representatives.Calilung told the senator-judges that he was the “agent on case”, and that his role was to gather evidence for the investigation into Duterte’s threats against Marcos, his wife First Lady Liza Araneta-Marcos, and his cousin former House Speaker Martin Romualdez.“It means that I viewed, identified, preserved, collected, and authenticated digital evidence in the course of the investigation,” Calilung said during direct examination.Among the evidence gathered by the NBI was the video of Duterte’s online press briefing, as shared on Facebook by former presidential spokesperson Harry Roque.The investigator testified that he authenticated the recording by comparing a screen-recorded copy with the original livestream.“After screen recording, there’s an output video. I compared the output video with the Nov. 23, 2024 video and ensured that the contents are identical,” he said.“After ensuring that the contents are identical, I generated a hash value calculation on the video to ensure that the video won’t be tampered until presented in court,” the witness said.After Calilung identified the relevant timestamp, prosecutors played the authenticated portion of the recording before the impeachment court.In the video, Duterte said: “Huwag kang mag-alala, ma’am, sa security ko, kasi may kinausap na ako na tao. Sinabi ko sa kanya, kapag pinatay ako, patayin mo si BBM (Bongbong Marcos), si Liza Araneta, at si Martin Romualdez. No joke. No joke. children.answer: Based on the evidence, **the NBI secured video recordings and conducted forensic authentication** of Vice President Sara Duterte's death threats. NBI Senior Agent John Mark Calilung testified before the Senate impeachment court, authenticating video evidence from November 23 and October 18, 2024, including detailing the forensic process of screen recording, hash value generation, and preservation requests to Meta (https://mb.com.ph/2026/07/07/nbi-witness-confirms-vp-sara-death-threat-video-in-impeachment-trial). The Philstar article mentions the NBI panel expects "full bureau reporting" beyond initial cybercrime findings testified to by NBI regional director Jeremy Lotoc (https://www.philstar.com/headlines/2026/07/17/2542784). **No specific evidence about seized physical documents or materials related to confidential funds allegations was found in the sources.** Confidence: Medium (strong evidence on threat recordings, but limited detail on confidential funds investigation materials).

Based on the evidence, the prosecution has disclosed: (1) **COA audit reports** including the P73 million disallowance notice and documentation of 1,200+ deficient acknowledgment receipts with erroneous dates and unreadable names; (2) **PSA certifications** showing no records for alleged recipients like "Mary Grace Piattos" and "Kokoy Villamin"; (3) **NBI handwriting analysis** of 36 receipts suggesting only seven individuals authored entries across documents; and (4) **authenticated video recordings** of VP Duterte's death threats from November 23 and October 18, 2024, with NBI testimony on forensic authentication including hash values and Meta preservation requests (sources: newsinfo.inquirer.net, www.congress.gov.ph, www.abs-cbn.com, mediabytesph.com, www.pna.gov.ph, mb.com.ph). Additionally, the Senate impeachment court has granted access to **subpoenaed bank and tax records** of VP Duterte and her husband (www.rappler.com). **Confidence: High** – multiple sources confirm these specific categories of evidence in official proceedings.

children.title: Legal Standards and Procedural Framework
children.question: What are the legal standards and procedural requirements for impeachment conviction in the Philippines?
children.direction: Review Philippine Constitution provisions on impeachment (Article XI), rules of the Senate sitting as impeachment court, evidentiary standards, voting requirements (2/3 of all members), and precedents from past impeachment cases.
children.success_criterion: Clear explanation of constitutional grounds, evidentiary thresholds, procedural rules, and voting requirements for conviction.

children.title: Evidentiary and Proof Standards
children.question: What standard of proof and evidentiary rules apply in impeachment trials?
children.direction: Research whether the standard is beyond reasonable doubt, preponderance of evidence, or substantial evidence; rules on admissibility; and how these standards have been applied in past Philippine impeachment cases.
research_areas: Political vs judicial nature of impeachment
children.success_criterion: Definition of applicable standard of proof and key evidentiary rules for impeachment proceedings.

SENATE OF THE PHILIPPINES sitting as an IMPEACHMENT COURT, BANK OF THE PHILIPPINE ISLANDS, PHILIPPINE SAVINGS BANK, ARLENE "KAKA" BAG-AO, GIORGIDI AGGABAO, MARILYN PRIMICIAS-AGABAS, NIEL TUPAS, RODOLFO FARINAS, SHERWIN TUGNA, RAUL DAZA, ELPIDIO BARZAGA, REYNALDO UMALI, NERI COLMENARES (ALSO KNOWN AS THE PROSECUTORS FROM THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES), Respondents.
R E S O L U T I O N
VILLARAMA, JR., J.:
Before this Court is a petition for certiorari and prohibition with prayer for immediate issuance of temporary restraining order (TRO) and writ of preliminary injunction filed by the former Chief Justice of this Court, Renato C. Corona, assailing the impeachment case initiated by the respondent Members of the House of Representatives (HOR) and trial being conducted by respondent Senate of the Philippines.
On December 12, 2011, a caucus was held by the majority bloc of the HOR during which a verified complaint for impeachment against petitioner was submitted by the leadership of the Committee on Justice. After a brief presentation, on the same day, the complaint was voted in session and 188 Members signed and endorsed it, way above the one-third vote required by the Constitution.
On December 13, 2011, the complaint was transmitted to the Senate which convened as an impeachment court the following day, December 14, 2011.
On December 15, 2011, petitioner received a copy of the complaint charging him with culpable violation of the Constitution, betrayal of public trust and graft and corruption, allegedly committed as follows:

ARTICLE I
RESPONDENT BETRAYED THE PUBLIC TRUST THROUGH HIS TRACK RECORD MARKED BY PARTIALITY AND SUBSERVIENCE IN CASES INVOLVING THE ARROYO ADMINISTRATION FROM THE TIME OF HIS APPOINTMENT AS SUPREME COURT JUSTICE AND UNTIL HIS DUBIOUS APPOINTMENT AS A MIDNIGHT CHIEF JUSTICE TO THE PRESENT.
ARTICLE II
RESPONDENT COMMITTED CULPABLE VIOLATION OF THE CONSTITUTION AND/OR BETRAYED THE PUBLIC TRUST WHEN HE FAILED TO DISCLOSE TO THE PUBLIC HIS STATEMENT OFASSETS, LIABILITIES AND NET WORTH AS REQUIRED UNDER SEC. 17, ART. XI OF THE 1987 CONSTITUTION.

2.1. It is provided for in Art. XI, Section 17 of the 1987 Constitution that "a public officer or employee shall, upon assumption of office and as often thereafter as may be required by law, submit a declaration under oath of his assets, liabilities, and net worth. In the case of the President, the Vice-President, the Members of the Cabinet, and other constitutional offices, and officers of the armed forces with general or flag rank, the declaration shall be disclosed to the public in the manner provided by law."
2.2. Respondent failed to disclose to the public his statement of assets, liabilities, and net worth as required by the Constitution.
2.3. It is also reported that some of the properties of Respondent are not included in his declaration of his assets, liabilities, and net worth, in violation of the anti-graft and corrupt practices act.

children.answer: According to the UP Law Impeachment Primer (cited in search results from https://law.upd.edu.ph/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/UP-Law-Impeachment-Primer-and-FAQs-Feb-7-2025.pdf), proof beyond reasonable doubt is not required for conviction in Philippine impeachment proceedings, unlike in criminal cases. Past Senates have also declined to apply a judicial standard of proof such as substantial evidence. The Senate's Rules of Procedure on Impeachment Trials (available at https://chanrobles.com/legal11impeachmentrules.htm) grant the presiding officer authority to rule on questions of evidence including materiality, relevancy, competency, and admissibility. Confidence: **Moderate** – evidence confirms no "beyond reasonable doubt" standard and Senate discretion over evidentiary rules, but specific standard applied remains unclear from available sources.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
 
 
 
 
 
2011–2012 Philippine charging of chief justice
Impeachment of Renato CoronaThe Senate of the Philippines convenes as the Impeachment CourtAccusedRenato Corona (Chief Justice of the Supreme Court of the Philippines)ProponentsRisa Hontiveros, Rodolfo Lozada Jr., Juan Carlo Tejano, and Lea Lopez NavarroDate12 December 2011 – 29 May 2012 (2011-12-12 – 2012-05-29)OutcomeConvicted by the Senate of the Philippines and removed from office.Charges

Impeachment vote by the House of Representatives (December 12, 2011)Votes in favor188 / 285 (66%)Not voting97 / 285 (34%)ResultImpeachment successfulDecision by Senate sitting as Impeachment Court (May 29, 2012)Votes in favor20 / 23 (87%)Votes against3 / 23 (13%)ResultCorona removed and perpetually disqualified from holding of public office.

Renato Corona, the 23rd chief justice of the Supreme Court of the Philippines, was impeached on December 12, 2011. Corona was the third official, after former President Joseph Estrada in 2000 and Ombudsman Merceditas Gutierrez in March 2011, to be impeached by the House of Representatives.[1]

The Senate, convened as an impeachment court, began the impeachment trial on January 16, 2012. This was the second impeachment trial in the history of the Philippines, as Gutierrez had resigned prior to the start of her trial. On May 29, 2012, Corona was found guilty of article two of the articles of impeachment that had been filed against him pertaining to his failure to disclose to the public his statement of assets, liabilities and net worth. He is the first public official who was removed and perpetually disqualified from holding public office.[2]

Appointment of Corona as chief justice[edit]
President Gloria Macapagal-Arroyo administers the oath of office to Supreme Court of the Philippines Chief Justice Renato C. Corona at the Malacanang Palace on May 17, 2010.

Chief Justice Reynato Puno was to retire on May 17, 2010, seven days after the presidential election. However, the constitution prohibits President Gloria Macapagal Arroyo from appointing anyone two months before the presidential election up to the end of her term. This caused a suit to be brought to the Supreme Court, in which the high tribunal ruled on March 17, 2010, that the ban on appointments does not cover the judiciary. The court ruled on the case with finality on April 20, 2010, with nine justices concurring, one dissenting and two dismissing the petition for being premature. Chief Justice Puno and Associate Justices Corona and Antonio Carpio abstained from ruling on the case. The court then ordered the Judicial and Bar Council (JBC) to proceed with its nomination process and subsequently submit its shortlist of nominees for the Chief Justice to Arroyo.[3]

Corona was appointed chief justice on May 12, 2010. He was the "most senior" Supreme Court justice among the four nominees of the JBC.[4]

With Benigno Aquino III winning the election, he invited all heads of the three branches of government to his inauguration, although instead of the tradition of him being inaugurated by the chief justice, he instead chose to be sworn in by Associate Justice Conchita Carpio-Morales, the sole dissenter on the case.[5]

Conflict between Aquino and Corona[edit]

CHIEF JUSTICE RENATO C. CORONA, Petitioner,
vs.
SENATE OF THE PHILIPPINES sitting as an IMPEACHMENT COURT, BANK OF THE PHILIPPINE ISLANDS, PHILIPPINE SAVINGS BANK, ARLENE "KAKA" BAG-AO, GIORGIDI AGGABAO, MARILYN PRIMICIAS-AGABAS, NIEL TUPAS, RODOLFO FARINAS, SHERWIN TUGNA, RAUL DAZA, ELPIDIO BARZAGA, REYNALDO UMALI, NERI COLMENARES (ALSO KNOWN AS THE PROSECUTORS FROM THE HOUSE OF REPRESENTATIVES), Respondents.
R E S O L U T I O N
VILLARAMA, JR., J.:
Before this Court is a petition for certiorari and prohibition with prayer for immediate issuance of temporary restraining order (TRO) and writ of preliminary injunction filed by the former Chief Justice of this Court, Renato C. Corona, assailing the impeachment case initiated by the respondent Members of the House of Representatives (HOR) and trial being conducted by respondent Senate of the Philippines.
On December 12, 2011, a caucus was held by the majority bloc of the HOR during which a verified complaint for impeachment against petitioner was submitted by the leadership of the Committee on Justice. After a brief presentation, on the same day, the complaint was voted in session and 188 Members signed and endorsed it, way above the one-third vote required by the Constitution.
On December 13, 2011, the complaint was transmitted to the Senate which convened as an impeachment court the following day, December 14, 2011.
On December 15, 2011, petitioner received a copy of the complaint charging him with culpable violation of the Constitution, betrayal of public trust and graft and corruption, allegedly committed as follows:

ARTICLE I
RESPONDENT BETRAYED THE PUBLIC TRUST THROUGH HIS TRACK RECORD MARKED BY PARTIALITY AND SUBSERVIENCE IN CASES INVOLVING THE ARROYO ADMINISTRATION FROM THE TIME OF HIS APPOINTMENT AS SUPREME COURT JUSTICE AND UNTIL HIS DUBIOUS APPOINTMENT AS A MIDNIGHT CHIEF JUSTICE TO THE PRESENT.
ARTICLE II
RESPONDENT COMMITTED CULPABLE VIOLATION OF THE CONSTITUTION AND/OR BETRAYED THE PUBLIC TRUST WHEN HE FAILED TO DISCLOSE TO THE PUBLIC HIS STATEMENT OFASSETS, LIABILITIES AND NET WORTH AS REQUIRED UNDER SEC. 17, ART. XI OF THE 1987 CONSTITUTION.

2.1. It is provided for in Art. XI, Section 17 of the 1987 Constitution that "a public officer or employee shall, upon assumption of office and as often thereafter as may be required by law, submit a declaration under oath of his assets, liabilities, and net worth. In the case of the President, the Vice-President, the Members of the Cabinet, and other constitutional offices, and officers of the armed forces with general or flag rank, the declaration shall be disclosed to the public in the manner provided by law."
2.2. Respondent failed to disclose to the public his statement of assets, liabilities, and net worth as required by the Constitution.
2.3. It is also reported that some of the properties of Respondent are not included in his declaration of his assets, liabilities, and net worth, in violation of the anti-graft and corrupt practices act.

children.answer: Based on the evidence, Philippine impeachment cases show a stark contrast in evidentiary outcomes: **Corona (2012) was convicted** by a Senate vote of 20-3 specifically for failing to disclose his Statement of Assets, Liabilities and Net Worth (SALN), particularly undisclosed bank deposits, constituting betrayal of public trust under Article 2 (Wikipedia/lawphil.net). In contrast, **Estrada's (2000-2001) impeachment was aborted** when the Senate voted to reject opening a second envelope of evidence, leading to his resignation during the resulting popular uprising rather than formal conviction (Wikipedia). **Sereno (2018) never faced impeachment trial** but was instead ousted via quo warranto by an 8-6 Supreme Court vote for allegedly failing to file SALNs during her law professorship years (Wikipedia). The evidence suggests that **documentary proof of SALN violations has been determinative** for conviction, while procedural barriers or alternative legal mechanisms prevented full trials in other cases. **Confidence: Medium-High** - the evidence directly addresses outcomes but lacks detailed comparative analysis of evidentiary standards across cases.

According to the UP Law Impeachment Primer (https://law.upd.edu.ph/wp-content/uploads/2025/02/UP-Law-Impeachment-Primer-and-FAQs-Feb-7-2025.pdf), Philippine impeachment conviction does **not require proof beyond reasonable doubt** as in criminal cases, and past Senates have declined to apply judicial standards like substantial evidence. The Senate Rules of Procedure on Impeachment Trials (https://chanrobles.com/legal11impeachmentrules.htm) grant the presiding officer discretion to rule on evidentiary questions including materiality, relevancy, competency, and admissibility. The **Corona conviction (2012)** demonstrates that documentary evidence of SALN violations can meet the evidentiary threshold for conviction, with a Senate vote of 20-3 establishing betrayal of public trust. **Confidence: Moderate** – sources confirm the absence of criminal proof standards and Senate procedural discretion, but the specific evidentiary threshold actually applied remains undefined in available evidence.

Senate of the Philippines 
 
 Duterte allies get key Senate chairmanships: What it means
 
 
 Jul 31, 2025 9:41 AM PHT 
 Bonz Magsambol
 
 

 

 
 
 

 
 
 
 WHY THIS MATTERS
 
 
 
 
 
 The Senate committee chairmanships for the 20th Congress were announced, rewarding supporters of Senate President Chiz Escudero and revealing the influence of the 'Duterte bloc' in securing key positions.
 Senator Rodante Marcoleta was appointed chair of the blue ribbon committee, raising concerns about the fairness of investigations involving the Duterte family, while other senators like Robin Padilla and Bong Go also received significant committee roles.
 Political analysts suggest that the 'Duterte bloc' could obstruct legislation and weaken President Marcos's influence in the Senate, complicating his ability to advance priority legislation.
 
 
 
 
 This is AI-generated and may have errors. For context, always refer to the full article. Report issues here. 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 NEW SET. A new set of senators for the 20th Congress officially starts their work on Monday, July 28. 
 
 Angie de Silva/Rappler 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 As expected, the 'supermajority' got first dibs on committees, but more telling is the 'Duterte bloc' securing key posts that could challenge the Marcos administration
 

 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
Political pundits called it “payback time” when the Senate committee chairmanships for the 20th Congress were announced on Tuesday, July 29. The leadership lineup showed that those who supported Senate President Chiz Escudero’s continued leadership were amply rewarded.

It was no surprise that the 19-member supermajority bloc got first dibs on the committees. What was more revealing, however, was how the so-called “Duterte bloc” secured key committees — positions that could make life difficult for the Marcos administration.

Beyond Escudero’s handling of the impeachment trial, powerful committees were handed to Duterte-allied senators — likely to secure their support. These include Rodante Marcoleta, Robin Padilla, Bato dela Rosa, Bong Go, Imee Marcos, and siblings Mark and Camille Villar. (READ: Chiz Escudero and his dilly-dallying over impeachment)

Marcoleta’s Blue ribbon

Neophyte Senator Rodante Marcoleta, who turned 72 on Tuesday, likely received his best birthday gift yet: the chairmanship of the powerful blue ribbon committee, the Senate panel responsible for high-profile investigations.
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

After winning the election, Marcoleta was vocal about his interest in chairing the blue ribbon committee. A month before the 20th Congress opened, Escudero and Senate President Pro Tempore Jinggoy Estrada met with Marcoleta over lunch on June 24. While the meeting was officially described as a casual catch-up, its timing raised not a few eyebrows.

Marcoleta, a member of the influential religious group Iglesia Ni Cristo (INC), was appointed to lead the committee over other veteran senators familiar with the workings of the Senate. The INC is a strong ally of the Duterte family, having opposed the arrest of former president Rodrigo Duterte and supported Vice President Sara Duterte amid the political challenges she was facing. He was also a key figure who pushed for the shutdown of ABS-CBN due to its critical coverage of the Duterte administration.

With the impeachment trial of the Vice President halted, Escudero said on Tuesday, July 29, that there are other venues through which the public can seek accountability from her.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia
 
 
 
 
 
2026 trial in the Philippine Senate
For the impeachments of Sara Duterte by the House of Representatives, see Impeachment of Sara Duterte.

Impeachment trial of Sara DuterteFrancis Escudero (right) presides over the impeachment trial of Sara Duterte, seated beside Senate president Sherwin Gatchalian (left)AccusedSara Duterte, Vice President of the PhilippinesPresiding officerFrancis EscuderoDateJuly 6, 2026 – present (2 weeks)Charges
Betrayal of public trust
Culpable violation of the Constitution
Graft and corruption
Bribery

The impeachment trial of Sara Duterte, the 15th vice president of the Philippines, began on July 6, 2026. Duterte had been impeached for a second time by the House of Representatives on May 11, 2026. She was the first vice president of the Philippines to be impeached, and the first to be impeached twice. The articles of impeachment alleged betrayal of public trust, culpable violation of the Constitution, graft and corruption, and bribery arising from the alleged misuse of the funds for the Office of the Vice President and the Department of Education (DepEd), where Duterte served as secretary from 2022 to 2024; unexplained wealth; alleged monetary gifts and payments to DepEd officials; and alleged threats to assassinate President Bongbong Marcos, First Lady Liza Araneta Marcos, and former House Speaker Martin Romualdez.[1]

What would have been Duterte's first Senate impeachment trial was postponed after the impeachment court announced that the proceedings would resume only after the prosecution panel had been reconstituted, following the loss of several members of the 19th Congress in the 2025 elections or their assumption of office in other government posts. The articles of impeachment were subsequently declared null and void by the Supreme Court in July 2025,[2] followed by the Senate impeachment court's decision to archive them in August 2025.[3]

Senate President Sherwin Gatchalian opened the session on the first day of the trial, after which he called for the election of a presiding officer. On June 3, 2026, the Senate had amended Rule II of its rules of procedure on impeachment trials to allow the election, by majority vote, of a senator other than the Senate president as the court's presiding officer. Francis Escudero, who had presided over Duterte's first impeachment trial as Senate president in 2025, was elected to the position.[4] The amendment was challenged by Senator-Judge Alan Peter Cayetano, who argued that the court could not amend a constitutional requirement that the Senate president preside over impeachment trials.[5] Several other minority senators likewise objected, delaying the start of the trial by approximately one hour.[6]

Members of the House prosecution panel include Representatives Gerville Luistro (lead prosecutor), Kaka Bag-ao, Joel Chua, Leila de Lima, Lorenz Defensor, Chel Diokno, Jonathan Keith Flores, Rodge Gutierrez, Terry Ridon, Lordan Suan, and Bel Zamora.[7] Duterte's defense team is led by litigator Sheila Sison.[8]

Sara Duterte impeachment 
 
 3 obstacles the prosecution must hurdle in Sara Duterte’s money trail
 
 
 Jul 20, 2026 8:00 AM PHT 
 Lance Spencer Yu
 
 

 

 
 
 

 
 
 
 WHY THIS MATTERS
 
 
 
 
 
 Prosecutors are seeking to investigate Vice President Sara Duterte's financial records to determine if she has undisclosed wealth, with plans to subpoena bank and tax records.
 The Senate must approve these subpoenas, but there are legal challenges regarding the release of tax records due to confidentiality laws, which may require presidential authority.
 Even if records are obtained, proving that Duterte's wealth is unexplained will be complex, as investigators will need to compare various financial documents to establish discrepancies.
 
 
 
 
 This is AI-generated and may have errors. For context, always refer to the full article. Report issues here. 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 MEET. Vice President Sara Duterte arrives at the Senate on July 7, 2026, to meet with her lawyers but did attend her impeachment trial. 
 
 Angie de Silva/Rappler 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 We explain the fight over the Vice President's bank and tax records
 

 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
MANILA, Philippines – To examine whether Vice President Sara Duterte accumulated wealth she failed to disclose or could not explain, prosecutors want to compare what she declared in her Statements of Assets, Liabilities and Net Worth (SALN), what moved through bank accounts linked to her, and what income she reported to tax authorities.

Senator-judges are expected to continue deliberation on Monday, July 20, whether to issue subpoenas for the financial records of Duterte and her husband, lawyer Manases Carpio.

The court held oral arguments on Wednesday, July 15, but emerged from a closed-door caucus lasting more than an hour without a ruling. The postponement suggests they haven’t reached a common position on whether the subpoenas are necessary fact-finding tools or an excessively broad “fishing expedition,” as Duterte’s defense argues.

What prosecutors want

The prosecution wants nine bank branches ordered to produce records relating to Duterte and Carpio, including accounts, deposits, investments, trusts, loans, safe-deposit boxes, and transactions allegedly maintained, controlled, beneficially owned, or held by the couple or their representatives, nominees, and signatories from 2007 to 2025.
 
 
 
 
 
 
 

It also wants the Anti-Money Laundering Council (AMLC) to turn over covered transaction reports and suspicious transaction reports involving the couple, along with related investigation documents and electronic database extracts. (LIST: Sara Duterte’s bank accounts, AMLC records covered by subpoena request)

The tax records are the subject of a separate subpoena request. During the House impeachment inquiry, the Bureau of Internal Revenue (BIR) produced a sealed box containing records involving Duterte, Carpio, and businesses linked to them.

The same box later traveled to the Senate before it was returned to the BIR unopened.

A subpoena would compel the BIR commissioner to appear and bring Duterte’s tax records, although separate presidential authority may still be required before they can be disclosed.

 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
 
 
 
 

 
 
 
So, what are the obstacles standing in the prosecution’s way?

children.answer: VP Duterte's defense argues the impeachment complaint is "not supported by evidence" and relies on speculation and fabricated claims, particularly rejecting allegations of actionable threats against President Marcos and his family (sunstar.com.ph, July 14, 2026). Counter-evidence challenges include legal obstacles to accessing her bank and tax records due to confidentiality laws that may require presidential authority, making it difficult for prosecutors to prove unexplained wealth by comparing financial documents (Rappler, July 20, 2026). The defense's strategy centers on attacking evidentiary weaknesses in the prosecution's case. **Confidence: Medium** - sources confirm defense statements and prosecution challenges, though detailed legal arguments from counter-affidavits were not accessible.

Based on the available evidence, I cannot provide a specific answer about what legal experts, analysts, and political observers currently assess regarding the prosecution's case strength. The search results reference historical impeachment proceedings and general legal commentary but do not contain expert assessments evaluating the strength of evidence in any current impeachment case. The only relevant evidence notes defense claims that the complaint is "not supported by evidence" (sunstar.com.ph, July 14, 2026), but this represents the defense position rather than independent expert analysis. **Confidence: Low** - no direct evidence addresses expert assessments of prosecution case strength.

root.answer:
Based on the evidence, the prosecution appears to have substantial documentation including **COA audit reports showing P73 million in disallowances, PSA certifications demonstrating fictitious fund recipients, NBI handwriting analysis suggesting fraudulent receipts, and authenticated video recordings of death threats** (newsinfo.inquirer.net, congress.gov.ph, abs-cbn.com, pna.gov.ph). Since Philippine impeachment **does not require proof beyond reasonable doubt** and the 2012 Corona case established that documentary evidence of violations can meet conviction thresholds with a 20-3 Senate vote (UP Law Impeachment Primer, Corona precedent), the prosecution's evidence inventory appears sufficient under the applicable legal standard. However, the **actual evidentiary threshold remains undefined** and subject to Senate discretion during trial proceedings.

**Confidence: Moderate** - evidence confirms substantial prosecution documentation and lower burden of proof, but Senate's ultimate evidentiary standard application is unpredictable.
